// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 5 — THE FIGMA AUDIT

Before writing any code, it helps to understand the structure of what you are building. The audit has three stages: fetc

Chapter 5 — The Figma Audit — The severity levels matter and they are not decorative. An error breaks or corrupts the pipeline — an orphaned alias, a
NARRATION

Before writing any code, it helps to understand the structure of what you are building. The audit has three stages: fetch the file data, apply a rule set to it, and emit findings. Each finding has a category, a severity, a reference to the Figma object that triggered it, and a suggestion for what to do. The severity levels matter and they are not decorative.

// SLIDE 02 — BUILDING FIGMA-AUDIT.JS

BUILDING FIGMA-AUDIT.JS

category'naming' — 'token-hygiene'
severity'error' — 'warning'
const isPrimitive = v.name.includes('/palette/') — v.name.includes('/scale/');
NARRATION

The architecture follows directly from the three-stage model. A main script fetches data and wires together check functions. Each check function receives the full data and returns an array of findings. A renderer emits the findings as both a human-readable markdown report and a machine-readable JSON file.

// SLIDE 03 — THE REPORT

THE REPORT

The report serves two audiences simultaneously: a human reading a markdown file in a browser or Slack, and a machine par

The Report — The markdown report exists so a designer can open it, find the specific node, click the deep link into Figma, and fix th
NARRATION

The report serves two audiences simultaneously: a human reading a markdown file in a browser or Slack, and a machine parsing JSON in CI. Both outputs are written on every run. The markdown report exists so a designer can open it, find the specific node, click the deep link into Figma, and fix the problem. The JSON report exists so CI can count errors, run the diff against the baseline, and decide whether to block the build.

// SLIDE 04 — THE RATCHET: BASELINES AND CI

THE RATCHET: BASELINES AND CI

There is a name for the discipline this audit enforces: it comes from database refactoring tools like Flyway and Liquiba

The Ratchet: Baselines and CI — On day one, a large legacy file may have hundreds of warnings. You cannot block CI on all of them — you would never merg
NARRATION

There is a name for the discipline this audit enforces: it comes from database refactoring tools like Flyway and Liquibase, which established the idea that schema changes must be forward-only, tracked in a version log, and applied idempotently. The audit baseline is the same concept applied to design quality. You commit where you are. You can only improve. Regressions are visible immediately. On day one, a large legacy file may have hundreds of warnings.

// SLIDE 05 — .GITHUB/WORKFLOWS/FIGMA-PIPELI

.GITHUB/WORKFLOWS/FIGMA-PIPELINE.YML (EXCERPT)

steps: name: Audit Figma file run: npm run figma:audit:ci env: FIGMA_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.FIGMA_TOKEN }} FIGMA_FILE_KEY: $

.github/workflows/figma-pipeline.yml (ex — name: Extract tokens (only if audit passes) if: success() run: npm run figma:tokens
NARRATION

steps: name: Audit Figma file run: npm run figma:audit:ci env: FIGMA_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.FIGMA_TOKEN }} FIGMA_FILE_KEY: ${{ secrets.FIGMA_FILE_KEY }} name: Extract tokens (only if audit passes) if: success() run: npm run figma:tokens

// SLIDE 06 — WHAT THE AUDIT CANNOT CATCH

WHAT THE AUDIT CANNOT CATCH.

Variable naming violations — Yes
Orphaned alias references — Yes
Static text/background contrast — Yes (single-frame pairs)
Semantic correctness of a name — No
NARRATION

The audit validates structure. It cannot validate intent. A variable named color/brand/primary that actually holds a secondary color passes every naming check. The audit did its job. The designer made a mistake. These are different problems and only one of them is machine-checkable. Accessibility beyond static contrast is not expressible in static Figma data. Hover states, focus rings, animation timing, motion sensitivity, keyboard navigation, focus management , none of this exists in the REST API response.

// SLIDE 07 — LINTERS AND THE AUTOMATED QUAL

LINTERS AND THE AUTOMATED QUALITY GATE

The Figma audit is structurally identical to a code linter, and that is not a coincidence — it is the application of for

Linters and the Automated Quality Gate — JSLint appeared in 2002. ESLint followed in 2013. Both made the same argument: code quality is checkable by machine, and
NARRATION

The Figma audit is structurally identical to a code linter, and that is not a coincidence , it is the application of forty years of automated quality gate thinking to a new artifact type. JSLint appeared in 2002. ESLint followed in 2013. Both made the same argument: code quality is checkable by machine, and the machine should check it before a human wastes time on review.

// SLIDE 08 — WHAT COMES NEXT

WHAT COMES NEXT.

Chapter 6 builds figma-fix-plugin/ — the Plugin API complement to the audit. The audit identifies what is broken. The fi

What Comes Next — You have a report. Now let's fix what it found.
NARRATION

Chapter 6 builds figma-fix-plugin/ , the Plugin API complement to the audit. The audit identifies what is broken. The fix plugin repairs it in bulk from inside Figma: renaming naming violations, resolving orphaned aliases, adding missing descriptions to components. The audit's JSON output is the fix plugin's input. The two tools are designed to be used together. You have a report. Now let's fix what it found.

// SLIDE 09 — FIGMA-TOOLS: AUDIT PIPELINE

FIGMA-TOOLS: AUDIT PIPELINE

·Any true false positive (intentional design flagged as a violation) = add a suppression pattern to check-component-hygie
·Any false negative (real problem not caught) = add a new rule or extend the check function.
If TOK001 errors are absent but your fixture has aliased variables, confirm the alias structure in the fixture is { type'VARIABLE_ALIAS', id: '<id>' } — a different shape will cause the orphan check to miss them.
NARRATION

figma-audit.js reads from fixtures/, writes to reports/. Never modify reports/audit-baseline.json directly , only update it via npm run figma:audit:baseline after a deliberate, reviewed reduction in findings. The baseline is a team contract; changing it requires explicit sign-off. What you're validating: The audit-report.json produced by figma-audit.js in Exercise 4, plus one of the check functions Claude Code generated.

// SLIDE 10 — THESIS
